content.problemStatement:
Alice has recently learned in her economics class that the market is said to be in equilibrium when the supply is equal to the demand.

Alice has market data for NNN time points in the form of two integer arrays SSS and DDD. Here, SiS_iSi​ denotes the supply at the ithi^{th}ith point of time and DiD_iDi​ denotes the demand at the ithi^{th}ith point of time, for each 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N.

Help Alice in finding out the number of time points at which the market was in equilibrium.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain an integer TTT — the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case contains three lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN, the length of the arrays SSS and DDD.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers S1,S2,…,SNS_1, S_2, \ldots , S_NS1​,S2​,…,SN​.
The third line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers D1,D2,…,DND_1, D_2, \ldots , D_ND1​,D2​,…,DN​.


Output Format
For each test case, output the number of time points at which the market was in equilibrium.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains integer N, the length of the arrays. Second line contains N space‑separated integers S1 … SN (supply). Third line contains N space‑separated integers D1 … DN (demand).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the number of indices i (1≤i≤N) such that Si equals Di. Each answer is printed on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: indices 3 and 4 have equal supply and demand, so answer is 2. Test case 2: only index 1 matches, so answer is 1.
All three positions have equal supply and demand, therefore the count is 3.
Only at position 3 (value 3) supply equals demand, so the answer is 1.

solution.approach: For each test case read N and the two arrays. Initialise a counter to zero. Loop i from 0 to N‑1 and increment the counter whenever S[i] == D[i]. After the loop output the counter. This scans each array once, which is optimal because every element must be examined at least once to know whether it matches.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(T·N)
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra besides the input arrays

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        S = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        D = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        cnt = sum(1 for s, d in zip(S, D) if s == d)
        out_lines.append(str(cnt))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
